Chapter 8
Nursing care and hospital management
Rebecca de Gier, Meredith Gordon, Adrian Smith and Kirsty Steel
Hospital ward
Essential equipment
The hospital ward should be situated in a quiet location 
away from potential predators, noise and practice traffic  ■  Absorbent and soft bedding/substrate.
(Figure 8.1). Ideally, a ward dedicated to exotic prey spe- ■  Hides.
cies should be provided. Prey species are likely to feel  ■  Heavy ceramic food and water bowls.
vulnerable when housed outside their familiar home envi- ■  Water dropper bottles.
ronment. Exposure to the sight, smell and noises associ- ■  Hay (timothy, oat), herbivore pellets, vegeta-
ated with predatory animals such as dogs and cats can  bles, grass and vitamin C supplement (see 
further increase stress and discomfort, and may have an  Chapter 2).
impact upon behaviour and appetite. ■  Syringe-tipped catheters and syringe-feeding 
formula (if assisted feeding is required).
■  The environmental temperature should be kept at 
16–24ºC with a humidity of 30–70%.
■  It is important that the lights in the ward can be 
dimmed or switched on and off separately to the 
rest of the hospital.
■  A day/night cycle should be established to ensure 
patients have 10–12 hours of darkness overnight. It 
should be noted that many guinea pigs will emerge 
from their hides at night to feed.
Guinea pig enclosure containing an appropriately sized 
8.2
box as a hide, as well as food and water.
(Courtesy of Anton Vets Ltd, Andover)
Flooring and substrates
Enclosures with a solid floor are ideal. Wire flooring should 
be avoided, as it can trap guinea pig feet and may increase 
Hospital ward for guinea pigs situated in a quiet location  the risk of pododermatitis due to uneven weightbearing. 
8.1
away from predators. Absorbent bedding (e.g. towels, fleece, incontinence pads 
(Courtesy of Anton Vets Ltd, Andover) or commercially available products such as VetBed®; 
newspaper is acceptable for short durations) of suitable 
Enclosures thickness should be provided to help keep immobilized 
The enclosure provided should be large enough to allow  patients dry and prevent urine scalding (Figure 8.3). This 
the guinea pig to freely move and turn around (Figure 8.2).  type of bedding also allows the quality and quantity of the 
It is recommended that the space provided should be at  urine and faeces produced to be monitored. Organic mate-
least 0.9 m2 per guinea pig. The enclosure should be easy  rial, such as straw or wood shavings, is not suitable in a 
to clean and disinfect. hospital environment.

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Absorbent bedding (e.g. VetBed®) of a suitable thickness 
8.3
can be used to line the floor of the enclosure.
Hides
A hide should always be provided (Figure 8.4). Disposable 
cardboard boxes are a practical and hygienic option. Reus-
able hides are available but require thorough cleaning 
between patients.
A dropper bottle can be placed within a cardboard tube 
8.5
and affixed to the side of the enclosure.
(a)
Exercise area
Although difficult in a hospital environment, access to an 
indoor run should be provided if possible. The run should 
be cleaned and disinfected between patients. Guinea pigs 
should be supervised at all times whilst using the run.
Environmental enrichment
Mental and physical stimulation can help reduce stress 
and provide enrichment. For example, soft toys and chew-
able items can be added to the enclosure to provide 
environmental enrichment and prevent the exhibition of 
inappropriate behaviours. In addition, provision of a small 
amount of timothy hay allows burrowing and also provides 
(b) a food source. This should be replaced every day.
The welfare benefits of some forms of enrichment (e.g. 
A hide should be provided within the guinea pig enclo-
8.4 soft toys) must be weighed against potential infection risk. 
sure. (a) Single-use cardboard boxes, reusable plastic 
hides and (b) reusable soft hides are available. Patients with a companion (see below) may have a reduced 
(a, Courtesy of Anton Vets Ltd, Andover) need for toys.

Chapter 8 • Nursing care and hospital management
Cleaning
Cleaning and disinfection of the enclosure and furniture 
should be performed regularly throughout the length of 
stay of the patient. A proprietary disinfectant (Figure 8.7) 
that is safe for small animals should be applied at the 
correct concentration for the correct length of time 
(according to guidance from the manufacturer). To mini-
mize handling of the guinea pig and the associated stress, 
where possible, a second enclosure should be made 
available for the patient to be housed in whilst cleaning 
is undertaken.
Infection control
Definitions
(a)
■  Infectious disease – a disease caused by a 
microorganism or parasite.
■  Contagious disease – an infectious disease 
that spreads from one animal to another.
■  Zoonotic disease – a contagious disease that 
spreads from animals to humans.
Guinea pigs can be affected by a number of infectious, 
contagious and zoonotic diseases (Figure 8.8). If a patient 
or bonded companion that has been co-admitted into the 
hospital exhibits clinical signs of an infectious and/or 
zoonotic disease, then the pair must be isolated and barrier 
nursed accordingly. Local biosecurity protocols and stand-
ard operating procedures for the isolation ward should be 
in place (e.g. the World Health Organization (WHO) hand-
(b) washing technique, provision of personal protective equip-
ment (PPE), and cleaning procedures specific to the 
(a, b) Guinea pigs are often kept in bonded pairs and 
8.6 enclosures and furniture contained within the ward). 
separation during hospitalization can be stressful. Where 
possible, bonded companions should be co-admitted to the  Maintaining excellent standards of hygiene is of paramount 
hospital with the patient. importance for infection control.

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
wrapping it in a towel, leaving only the rear exposed, and 
the thermometer should be carefully inserted just a few 
millimetres into the rectum. The thermometer should be 
held steady until it beeps, then removed and read. A 
healthy guinea pig should have a rectal temperature 
between 37.2°C and 39.5°C.
Behaviour
The behaviour of the guinea pig should be assessed on a 
daily basis. Guinea pigs have a range of sounds that they 
use to communicate and each sound has a different mean-
ing (Figure 8.12). A basic understanding of guinea pig 
behaviour and vocalizations can help with the care and 
treatment of them as patients.
Handling and restraint
Handling

cated to help guinea pigs develop a positive association

Chapter 8 • Nursing care and hospital management
of the abdomen. Dorsal recumbency should be avoided 
as much as possible in patients with respiratory issues or 
with conditions that reduce thoracic volume (such as 
obesity or bloat). If dorsal recumbency cannot be avoided 
and the patient’s respiration is or becomes compromised, 
oxygen supplementation before, during and after the 
restraint is recommended.
Distraction in the form of gentle head stroking can 
be effective and offering high value food items (e.g. fresh 
herbs, grass or green vegetables) may be useful as 
guinea pigs will often eat enthusiastically even when 
under duress. However, food distractions may not be 
appropriate if the patient is to be anaesthetized for an 
examination or procedure due to the potential for fluid 
reflux and oesophagitis.
Restraint
Careful consideration should be given to restraining guinea 
pigs safely and appropriately whilst maintaining normal 
respiratory function and body temperature as well as 
minimizing discomfort and distress.
Physical restraint: Towels or blankets can be used to  (a)
provide gentle restraint by wrapping, covering and sup-
porting the guinea pig (Figure 8.14). Many patients appear 
calmer and less stressed if given the opportunity to hide 
their head under a light covering (such as a tea towel) or 
against the body of the handler.
Chemical restraint: For minor, non-invasive procedures 
such as radiography and ultrasonography, a multimodal 
combination of sedation and analgesia (e.g. buprenor-
phine and midazolam; see Chapter 7) administered via 
injection up to an hour prior to the procedure will increase 
compliance and reduce stress for the patient. The sedated 
patient should be placed in a clean enclosure that allows 
close visual monitoring and oxygen supplementation if 
required. Food and water should be removed; however, it 
(b)
Guinea pigs can be restrained using blankets for (a) hand 
8.14
feeding and (b) oral examination.
should be noted that some patients may eat their bedding 
in the absence of food. Body temperature must be main-
tained, so additional heating is appropriate for the majority 
of patients requiring sedation; the environmental tem-
perature should be monitored and adjusted as needed. 
The airways should remain clear, so the mouth should be 
gently cleaned with cotton tips to prevent the aspiration 
of food and saliva.
Topical local anaesthesia should be considered when 
performing procedures such as venepuncture, intravenous 
catheter placement or collecting cytology samples for 
examination. The application of a topical anaesthetic (e.g. 
prilocaine and lidocaine) to clean skin at least 45 minutes 
prior to the procedure can assist with patient compliance. 
There are some cases where general anaesthesia is nec-
essary to ensure the safe and complete restraint of an 
uncooperative patient (see Chapter 7). The benefits of 
being able to perform a procedure quickly, accurately and 
A guinea pig being restrained in dorsal recumbency for 
8.13 ultrasonography. Note that the thorax is elevated to avoid  with minimal restraints must be weighed against the risks 
compression of the lungs. associated with general anaesthesia.

where intravenous access cannot be achieved due to 
cardiovascular collapse or peripheral vasoconstriction.  Pain assessment
A pain assessment should be carried out for each patient. 
However, recognizing the clinical signs of pain and dis-
comfort can be challenging as guinea pigs are crepus-
cular and a prey species, meaning that they are often 
quiet during the day and prone to hiding signs of discom-
fort for as long as possible. The following signs may 
indicate illness, discomfort and pain:
■  Behavioural changes (e.g. lethargy, altered sleep 
patterns)
■  Reduced appetite
■  Reduced urine and faecal output
■  Vocalization during urination or defecation
■  Reduced grooming, resulting in a rough or unkempt 
hair coat
■  Excessive grooming or self-trauma
■  Squinting/almond-shaped eyes and ocular discharge
■  Change in whisker position and/or cheek bulging
8.16 Administration of subcutaneous fluids. ■  Flattened ears (Figure 8.18)
■  Bruxism/tooth grinding/chattering and excessive 
saliva evident around the mouth
■  Hunched/abnormal posture
■  Tense muscles and guarding of the abdomen
■  Flinching or vocalization on handling and palpation
■  Increased respiratory rate and effort
■  Changes in gait or decreased mobility
■  Increase or decrease in body temperature.
Worsening of these clinical signs should prompt reas-
sessment by the veterinary surgeon. Trial treatment with 
(non-sedative) analgesia may be helpful.
Nursing considerations for specific 
conditions
Guinea pigs being treated for specific acute or chronic 
conditions will have specific nursing requirements. An 
Hospitalized guinea pig with an intravenous catheter 
8.17 placed in the cephalic vein to allow administration of  assessment should be carried out and an individualized 
fluids via a syringe driver. nursing care plan generated for each patient.

tory distress. Any underlying disease processes should 
be assessed and managed accordingly as these can 
exacerbate respiratory compromise. A pain assessment 
should be carried out at regular intervals and medication 
adjusted as needed.
The patient should be housed in a quiet ward. The 
temperature and humidity in the enclosure should be 
monitored for spikes and to ensure that there are no 
draughts. Dust- and fragrance-free bedding should be 
used to help prevent aggravation of respiratory conditions. 
In addition, the high levels of ammonia present in the urine 
of guinea pigs can cause or exacerbate respiratory condi-
tions, so enclosures should be cleaned daily and bedding 
replaced as required.
Oxygen supplementation should be provided as 
needed. In cases of severe or acute respiratory distress, 
an oxygen mask can be held gently over the mouth and 
nose of the guinea pig. Flow-by oxygen placed under the 
nose of the guinea pig may also be useful in these 
instances. However, it should be remembered that the 
level of handling required for this type of oxygen sup-
plementation may be too stressful for some animals, and

Wound management: During the recovery period follow-
ing a surgical procedure, the factors below should be taken 
into consideration:
■  An enclosure with non-irritant, absorbent bedding 
should be provided. Straw and aspen shavings 
should be avoided as they can contaminate the 
wound, leading to irritation, patient interference and 
infection
■  The wound should be checked regularly for soiling, 
self-trauma by the patient and complications with 
the healing process.
Surgical wounds may be closed using suture material, 
staples or tissue glue (see Chapter 12). Wound healing
